title: Reading Stack Traces
intro: Your app just crashed and the console is showing a scary wall of text called a 'stack trace.' Imagine it like a trail of breadcrumbs through a maze -- each line shows you one step closer to where things went wrong. Don't panic. Let's learn to read it.

teach.title: A Stack Trace Is Like a Trail of Breadcrumbs
teach.body: Imagine you walk through five rooms and trip in the last one. A stack trace is like a list of every room you walked through, in order, with the room you tripped in listed first. It shows the chain of function calls that led to the crash.

teach.title: A Stack Trace Is Like a Call History on Your Phone
teach.body: When you miss a call and want to know who called, you check your call history -- it shows the most recent call at the top. A stack trace is your code's call history. The most recent function call (where the crash happened) is at the top, and each line below shows who called whom, leading all the way back to where things started.

teach.title: How to Read a Stack Trace
teach.body:
A stack trace reads **top to bottom**:

1. The **top line** is where the error actually happened -- start here
2. Each line below it shows which function called the one above it
3. **Skip framework lines** (like react-dom or node internals) -- focus on YOUR files

The top line of your own code is almost always where the bug is.

teach.title: A Real Stack Trace
teach.code:
TypeError: Cannot read properties of null
    at UserCard (src/UserCard.jsx:8)      <-- YOUR code, start here
    at renderWithHooks (react-dom.js:14985)  <-- framework, skip
    at App (src/App.jsx:22)               <-- YOUR code, called UserCard
teach.annotation: Start at the top line that mentions YOUR files (UserCard.jsx:8). That's where the crash happened. App.jsx:22 is where UserCard was called from.

teach.body: Stack traces look intimidating, but reading them is a superpower. Vibecoding pros who can decode a stack trace fix crashes in minutes instead of hours -- and that efficiency is what makes building and selling apps profitable.
Read stack traces from the top. The first line with your own file name is where the bug is. Skip lines from frameworks and libraries -- the problem is almost never in their code.

start.narrative:
Your React app crashes with this error:

TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'map')
    at ProductList (src/components/ProductList.jsx:12)
    at renderWithHooks (react-dom.development.js:14985)
    at mountIndeterminateComponent (react-dom.development.js:17811)
    at App (src/App.jsx:8)

Where do you look first?

choices.text: The top of the stack trace: ProductList.jsx line 12
The bottom of the stack trace: App.jsx line 8
The react-dom lines -- the error is probably in React itself
The 'products' variable is null when this line runs
The <li> element is causing the crash

wrong-bottom.narrative: Stack traces read top-to-bottom, where the top is the most recent call -- the place where the error actually happened. The bottom is where the chain of function calls started. Start at the top and look for YOUR files (not framework code).

choices.text: OK, let me look at the top: ProductList.jsx line 12

wrong-framework.narrative: Lines from node_modules or framework files like react-dom are almost never the source of your bug. They show up in the trace because your code called into the framework, which then hit the error. Skip framework lines and look for YOUR source files.

choices.text: OK, let me focus on my own files in the trace

found-null.narrative: Exactly! The error message 'Cannot read properties of null (reading 'map')' is telling you that you called .map() on something that is null. The variable 'products' is null. Now, how do you fix this?

choices.text: Add a check: if products is null, show a loading state or empty list
Initialize products to an empty array as a default
Wrap it in a try-catch block

wrong-map.narrative: The .map() function works fine -- it's a built-in JavaScript array method. The problem is what you're calling .map() ON. The error says you're calling .map() on null, meaning the variable before .map() has a null value.

choices.text: So 'products' must be null when this line runs

wrong-li.narrative: The JSX elements inside .map() aren't the problem. The crash happens before the .map() callback even runs. Look at the error message again: it says 'null' has no property 'map'. The thing you're calling .map() on is null.

choices.text: So the products variable is null

end-good.feedback: Great debugging! You read the stack trace top-to-bottom, focused on your own files (not framework code), decoded the error message to understand that 'products' was null, and added a proper guard check. This pattern -- checking for null before using data -- is something you'll do constantly in real apps.

end-okay.narrative: Setting a default empty array (const items = (products || []).map(...)) prevents the crash. But it might hide the fact that your data hasn't loaded yet. A loading state would give users better feedback.
end-okay.feedback: You correctly identified the null variable and prevented the crash. The default empty array works, but a loading state would be even better for user experience. Still, solid debugging -- you read the stack trace, found the right file and line, and understood the null issue.

end-trycatch.narrative: A try-catch hides the error but doesn't actually fix it. The component still won't show any products. It's better to handle the null case explicitly with a loading state or default value.
end-trycatch.feedback: You found the right problem, but try-catch is usually a band-aid. It silences the error without actually handling the null state. Next time, add a proper check for null and show a meaningful fallback (like a loading indicator) instead of just catching and ignoring the error.
